sentences.text: We were, we were liberated by the Russians.
They behaved, with us, really, very, very nice.
They opened all the storerooms with clothes, with food, and we could take anything we wanted.
We didn't know, two days before, that anything-- that, that the, that the war is finished, that we going to-- we might be liberated.
Because if-- I never-- you know, as much as I always was optimistic, I never had really hope of-- a real hope of surviving.
Because I thought like this-- if the German win the war, they will keep us here working.
They need us as-- till we fall and we, we die of, of, of starvation, of, of, of weakness, of anything.
And if they win the war, they kill us before.
I, I, I didn't want to think.
I didn't want to think.
I did not think that I'd survive, because there was no-- how, how could I think that I survive?
I felt-- the, the, the war is on, and, and the German will never let me free if they win the war.
And if they want to win the war, they'll kill me.
But I, I, I tried not to think about it.
I, I believe in God?
I believe in destination.
When I survived, I thought my destiny was to survive.
How could you-- how, how, how could you hope against every-- against any, any reason?
I was resigned, resigned.
But still, every free moment, we sang.
Would you believe it?
That the songs kept us.
The-- one was teaching the other one, singing this song and that song.
I had quite a repertoire of songs when I left the camp.
Any time, we were singing.
No.
When we were working, everybody worked for himself.
No.
It all depends what shift I had.
Let's say I had a shift at half past 1:00 in the morning or half past 1:00 in the afternoon.
So I suppose we got some soup.
We used to get a, a loaf of bread for the week, full of mildew.
Some girls used to eat it up in one day.
I used to always cut it up.
And I used to-- that piece of bread that I should eat that day, I used to eat in my bed at night.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I don't know, I sliced.
And I, I kept my bread together with [NON-ENGLISH].
If she was more hungry, she could eat it.
If I was more hungry, I was eating-- and her sister.
But with her sister, I wasn't so friendly.
I was only with [NON-ENGLISH].
We kept everything together.
I don't remember.
Maybe.
We had like a, a little wooden cupboard or something like that where we kept our clothes or something like that.
There was bread and everything.
Some people kept it under the pillow.
Yeah.
A piece every day, yes.
We used to get some soup before we left for work and when we came from work.
And we-- sometimes we used to get a bit of butter, sometimes a bit of jam, sometimes nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you know what, also, the Sudeten-- from Sudeten [NON-ENGLISH] you know, the Germans from Sudeten [NON-ENGLISH]-- some of them were fellow Semitics.
And some, you know, had their special girl.
I always was lucky.
Somebody liked me and pushed me a piece of bread or a piece of cake.
They were-- we were working in one hall.
Yes, that's right.
They were not working 12 hours, but they were working there.
I went to see them after the war finished.
Yes, the one who helped me.
Gave me a piece of bread, whatever they could, you know.
That was the only way they could help us, because they were in danger, too, if, if they would be caught.
Yeah.
We got, again, something to eat, something-- soup or something like this.
We were in the-- If it was morning, we used to get black coffee, no soup.
And, and if it was evening, we used to get a soup.
If, if I worked the shift from 6 o'clock in the morning to 6 o'clock at night, we got a soup.
Yeah.
It all depends what time it was.
If it-- if we had a shift from 6 o'clock at night to 6 o'clock in the morning, we were supposed to sleep for a little while.
But we, we-- they didn't, didn't let us sleep more than two, three hours.
Scrub the floors, bring the coal, peel the potatoes, go for messages, all sorts of things.
The Judenalteste.
Her name was [NON-ENGLISH].
Jewish.
I think that she prayed to God that the war never finish, because she never had it so good in her life.
I think she was very, very upset when the war finished.
I can't say anything really bad about her, but I can't say anything good about her.
No.
The Lagerfuhrerin beat up-- beat us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think that she-- maybe she beat, but she didn't beat me.
I don't remember her beating me-- maybe once.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
At first, we were only a few hundred.
But then suddenly, in 1944, all the Hungarian people came.
Hungarian women came from Auschwitz.
And then when we found out that Auschwitz exists.
Nothing.
Not exactly.
But they came.
They were all shaven.
They all had the same uniform.
You couldn't recognize who is the mother and who is the daughter, who was beautiful and who was ugly.
They all looked the same.
They were much worse off than we.
But slowly, slowly, they were integrated and went to work.
They were the lucky ones who came to us.
They had numbers.
They didn't-- they most-- they, they told us there are ovens and that people are being-- we don't-- I didn't want to listen to it, because I always had my mother in front of me.
I didn't want to know.
I thought that she was taken, taken to a camp somewhere.
But after the war-- shall I come to this-- I found out that in 19-- that the day I was taken to concentration camp, my brother died of typhoid.
He lived in Tomashov, where his wife comes from.
And he contracted typhoid, and he even got cured from it.
And his wife was sitting with him one day.
And the nurse passed by-- and he was already reconvalesced and everything-- and a nurse passed by with an injection.
And she said, what for are you giving him an injection?
And she said, for this infection.
And-- His wife asked the question?
Yeah.
That, I was told by people, by cousins which survived.
And when she, when she left, when she-- after a few minutes, she went, she went out of the room.
When she came back, he was dead.
A fatal injection.
And my mother never find out, found out that my brother is not alive.
And my sister-in-law wrote to me once, to the camp.
And she said, what's going to happen when [NON-ENGLISH] find out that you are in a camp?
You know, I didn't know nothing.
And later on, apparently, she sent the child, which you see in the photo here, to my mother.
Because where she was was, again, a [NON-ENGLISH].
And where my mother was-- Das Drittes Reich.
And somehow they had the funny idea that they have a better chance to survive in the Drittes Reich.
So that little boy was born in 1936.
He must've been, in 1943, six, seven years.
In, in 19-- it must have been in 1942 that he came to my mother.
Because my cousin was telling me that he waited till my mother leaves somewhere on messages, then he said Kaddish.
So then my mother never finds out that he, that he's-- that her son is dead.
I didn't have to find out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She went to Auschwitz.
With her.
And the little boy, apparently, when he was-- it, it was-- it was a ghetto already.
And it was cold.
It was winter.
And my cousin was telling me that he came to see him and brought him a piece of chocolate.
And he said, I don't want any chocolate.
My mother hasn't got any chocolate, either.
See, these things like, like this, they stick with you forever.
No.
Nobody survived.
Nobody.
I, I also, I-- my father had-- was one of seven.
My mother was one of four.
Each of them had big families-- seven, four, five.
After the war, I had one cousin in Switzerland, one cousin in Frankfurt, one cousin in Montreal, and two cousins in Israel.
Out of 10, one is alive.
In 1968, I went to Israel for the first time.
And that was the reunion of all of us.
Yeah.
Nobody.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
No, nothing.
Nothing.
When we were, were working at the trenches, the, the leaflets were coming down.
And some people would pick up the leaflets.

They said-- on one side it was written in, in German, and on one side it was written in English, to us in English, to the Germans in German-- to, to us, it was written to keep strong, that they are coming soon, that they are going to liberate us.
But we didn't believe this.
Yeah, yeah.
We were not allowed to pick up the leaflets.
Maybe April.
Maybe--
Only in 1945.
A few months.
Yes.
They were-- or some were working in the spinning.
The strong ones were going to the trenches.
Were dropped.
But we didn't-- we didn't know.
We didn't know nothing.
We didn't know that, Stalingrad, that they, they have, that they, they, they losing so many people in Russia that this-- that-- we didn't know anything of what happened in the world, nothing.
No.
No, no, no.
They were only SS men.
Just guarded us.
As defenses for the Germans.
So we thought, how can the war be finished soon if we're just making the trenches for them?
No.
No, I was just told.
Mm-hmm.
The war finished very sudden for me.
And I tell you what, I was-- I couldn't believe it.
I could not believe it, that I got liberated, that I can walk out.
The Russians.
Live in the barracks.
And one day, there was-- the, the Lagerfuhrerin vanished.
The SS group vanished.
There was nobody one day, not the Russian, not the German.
My guards disappeared.
Oh, the, the camp?
They disappeared, too.
The, the Judenalteste, I don't remember.
But I remember this Erma going.
We had one-- one of our inmates was a-- from a-- very frum.
You know?
And we-- I think she was the daughter of a rabbi.
And she went to her-- she was working as a tailoress.
And she said, [NON-ENGLISH], pray for me.
Yeah.
The, the, yeah, the Lagerfuhrerin.
She ran away, but they caught her.
I don't know much about it, because I didn't want-- I, I, I didn't want any revenge.
I didn't want to see anything.
I was just happy to be, to be free.
Yeah.
The Russians.
The Russians arrived.
They arrived, and they said that we can go out.
And they opened all the stores and to take food and to take clothes, and-- and, you know, there was medicine for the sick ones.
And, and, and there was nobody in the camp, you know, nobody.
We could do what we wanted.
I, I wanted, I wanted to find somebody.
I, I wanted to know if anybody of my people are alive.
So and I saw that were coming to our camps also, boys from other camps, you know, that they survived and are looking for sisters, for somebody.
And once, a group of them came.
And I asked, look, do you know anybody by the name Majerczyk?
He said, yes, in Waldenburg.
So not thinking of nothing, I left [NON-ENGLISH].
I left everybody, and I hitchhiked to Waldenburg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there I found two cousins of mine.
And they lived over there in a, in a flat.
In Waldenburg?
In Waldenburg.
That was still May, 1945.
I stayed with them.
And there I met my husband.
My husband comes from Vienna.
And he will, he will tell you his story separately.
But as it happened, he was in the concentration camp together with my cousins.
But I didn't meet him through my cousins.
I met him through an inmate of the concentration camp who also found himself in Waldenburg.
I met him there and, somehow, we must have liked each other.
We met again and again.
And then he said that he wants to go back to France because he was taken from France to the concentration camp.
He was there in the Foreign Legion.
That's his story.
He will tell you that.
Yeah.
We, we, we lived in Prague, in a, in a French colony where about 50 people, on one floor, slept.
And in the, in the train, we met some-- another friend of ours, who-- my husband's friend from Vienna, and we traveled together.
So the four of us lived together in that French colony.
And my husband tried to get a visa or, or whatever documents to go to, to France.
And they said, all right, we'll give it to you, but we can't give it to your girlfriend because she never comes from France.
So he tried the Austrian consulate to go back to Vienna.
The same-- you can go back, but she can't go back.
So we decided we're getting married.
So we knew each other 18 days, and we got married.
The wedding-- we had the, the two, the two-- the, the one of our witnesses is our best friend till today, but we've never seen him for many, many years.
We met again in Australia.
So he, he was the witness, [NON-ENGLISH], and my husband's friend [NON-ENGLISH].
They were-- we went and we performed the ceremony, and we got married.
And we're going-- we were in a great hurry to go back and get something to eat.
Because where, where, where would-- did we eat?
We stand in-- we stood in a queue for, for Red, Red Cross, or [NON-ENGLISH] or railway.
And we knew what's, what's given today here and what was given today here.
And we ate, three times, breakfast and, three times, lunch and, three times, dinner.
And we didn't need any money to travel, because they seen that we are from the camps.
On the train, on the bus, on the railway, we could go anywhere we wanted with nothing.
I think this part of the story, I leave for my husband, because he--
Yeah.
In Prague.
And we went back to this big hall, and we slept on the floor with the 50 people, together.
And when we were there, they-- and-- we, we were given some money.
Everybody was given-- from the concentration camps-- some money.
Wedding breakfast.
Potato salad and bread with red sugar.
This is, this is-- now I'll tell you the story from Prague.
When, when we-- then we got married, and my husband went to the Austrian consulate.
And we got permission to go, on this and this day, back to Vienna.
That was another few days to wait, so we stayed together with that group of people that we were in the French colony.
And one day, a Frenchman comes in, and he calls to my Walter, Walter, Walter.
And that was his commander when he was in the French army.
And he recognized him.
And he said, what are you doing here, and who is that blonde?
He said, we just got married.
And you live here?
So he said, we have no money to go anywhere else.
So he said, come on with me.
He booked us in, in the top hotel in Prague.
I, I remember, the room was green.
Everything-- the bedspread, the furniture-- it was beautiful.
And we were supposed to stay there the three days till our, our transport to Vienna goes.
So we stayed in, in that hotel the three days.
And on the third day, he was supposed to come and pay for us.
The third day comes, he doesn't come.
And we can't get out of here.
We didn't have any luggage.
We had a toothbrush.
And my husband, I think, had a tie.
And that's all that we possessed.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he didn't come, and he didn't come.
In the evening, suddenly, he is here.
So he says to my husband, I lost all my money.
I went to the races or-- I don't know.
I haven't got any money, but don't worry.
So he went to the, to the, to the counter and said, I'm taking over the room of Mr. and Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH].
That was our name then.
But they knew, already, what it is.
So they said, no, that room is taken.
So he took out his identification [INAUDIBLE].
And he was one of the top men in the underground.
So they let us go.
As it happened, we never reached Vienna.
Because we went on the train to Vienna.
We sit, sat in the train.
It was hours and hours that we sat there.
And we were waiting for the, for the papers to come.
And after hours, the papers came.
Everybody's came.
There was a big transport to Vienna.
Everybody's papers came, but not mine.
So I said to my husband, you go, and I'll go by the next transport to Vienna.
But he wouldn't leave me.
So we went back to the German-- Austrian consulate.
We got there a little room.
And my husband got a job on the German consulate.
On the Austrian.
Austrian-- on the Austrian consulate.
He was-- I don't know.
That's his story, so--
He worked for them.
For quite a few months.
And we stayed.
We never, never went to Vienna in the meantime.
I was going to the Red Cross and here and there to contact his mother, his sister.
His sister was-- one sister was in Australia, and one-- and his mother and his other sister were in America.
And after months and months, we got in touch with them.
So it was a--
And we decided that we'd go wherever we go-- wherever we get the, the permit or the affidavit first.
There, we'd go.
So we got the permit to Australia, so we were lucky enough to come here.
Oh, yes.
Nobody left.
When I met my cousins in Waldenburg--
--they already knew.
One of my cousins went back to the city in Tomashov with me.
And there, I got the confirmation that my, my brother died of typhoid on that and that day.
And that's why I'm able, every day, to, to put a candle on, on his yahrzeit--
--to know when his yahrzeit is.
Yes, I have a date.
And my mother's date, that yahrzeit I made at Yom Kippur.
Yeah.
Nothing.
Waited.
Nothing.
My husband was earning quite good money, because he was going to Vienna to get naturalization papers for some Germans which lost them, you know, from Austria, which lost them.
And that was his job, going backwards and forwards to Vienna.
I never went to Vienna.
First, we lived on the Austrian consulate.
And then we got a, a little, a, a room at somebody's-- you know, somebody had a big flat, a, a, a Czech woman.
And she gave us a room, and we stayed in the room till the day we left for France to catch the, the ship to Australia.
It was sometimes in June.
